summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that arbitrary code execution is achievable through unsafe pickle deserialization in CocoIndex's subprocess executor, demonstrating that attacker-controlled spec objects can trigger malicious code during unpickling.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should demonstrate that a malicious spec object with a crafted __reduce__ method executes arbitrary shell commands during pickle deserialization, creating a proof file to verify successful code execution.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the Docker image and executed the exploit script, which successfully created the proof file /tmp/evohunt_poc_success.txt with the expected message, confirming arbitrary code execution in the subprocess context.
verification_method: The exploit script creates a malicious spec class with a __reduce__ method that executes a shell command. After pickle.loads() is called on the malicious payload, the verification script checks for the existence and content of the proof file. This distinguishes successful code execution from a simple pickle deserialization failure or unrelated error.
evidence_produced_by_run: Container logs show the exploit script successfully creating and executing the malicious pickle payload.
The proof file /tmp/evohunt_poc_success.txt was created inside the container with the expected message.
The verification script confirmed the proof file exists and contains the correct content.
The exploit demonstrates that pickle.loads() on attacker-controlled data leads to arbitrary code execution.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh uses the pre-existing target repository at /Users/mac/ResearchSpace/opencode/EvoHunt-DryRun/advisory_5df94a99/repo and mounts it read-only into the container at /target-repo.
